title: Coconut and red lentil soup
subtitle: Spiced, creamy, and ready in 30 minutes
excerpt: Red lentils dissolved into coconut milk and stock, spiced with turmeric, cumin, and fresh ginger. A thick, bright yellow soup that is warming without being heavy, and good enough to eat every week.
sourceNotes: Red lentil and coconut soup draws on South Indian dal traditions, particularly the tarka dal approach of blooming spices in fat and combining them with dissolved red lentils and coconut milk. Method synthesised for a British home kitchen.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before adding the coconut milk for a cleaner result. Keeps for up to three months. Add coconut milk when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well and is an excellent batch-cook soup.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to three days ahead. Thickens on standing; thin with hot water when reheating.

glossaryTerms.definition: A finishing technique from South Asian cooking where spices are briefly fried in hot fat until fragrant, then poured over a cooked dish. The sizzling, spice-scented oil adds a final layer of flavour. Sometimes made at the start as a spiced base rather than as a finish.

content.text: Red lentils dissolve completely when cooked, thickening the soup without any blending. The spice base here follows the logic of a

: cook the onion, then bloom the spices in the fat before any liquid goes in. The turmeric gives the soup its colour; the cumin and ginger give it warmth. Coconut milk goes in at the end to keep it creamy without curdling. This is a soup that can be on the table in 30 minutes.

Heat {{coconut-oil}} of oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{ginger-fresh}} of grated ginger and cook for 1 minute.
Add {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes. Stir for 1 minute until fragrant. Add {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for 30 seconds.

Add the {{lentils-red}} of rinsed red lentils and pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the lentils have dissolved into the soup.
Stir in {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and the juice of {{lime}} lime. Simmer for 2 minutes. Taste and season with salt.
Ladle into bowls. Scatter coriander leaves over each bowl if using.

items.cause: Red lentils absorb a significant amount of liquid as they cook and thicken further on standing.
items.fix: Add hot water or stock a ladleful at a time. The soup should pour easily from a ladle. When reheating from the fridge, always add water.

items.symptom: Coconut milk has curdled into white specks
items.cause: The coconut milk was added to a vigorously boiling soup.
items.fix: Add coconut milk after reducing the heat to a gentle simmer. Full-fat coconut milk curdles less readily than light.

items.symptom: Turmeric taste is strong or medicinal
items.cause: Too much turmeric for the volume of lentils, or an old jar with a strong character.
items.fix: Turmeric is primarily for colour. 1 tsp is enough for 200 g of lentils. Add more cumin to balance if the turmeric is prominent.

items.cause: Lime juice and salt were not added at the end.
items.fix: The lime juice is essential and cannot be skipped. It brightens the soup and balances the richness of the coconut milk. Taste before serving and add more lime if needed.

content.text: Red lentil soups spiced with cumin, turmeric, and ginger are found across South Asia, the Middle East, and Sri Lanka. The coconut milk element comes from South Indian and Sri Lankan cooking, where coconut milk is used in both dal and soups. This version became widely popular in Britain in the 2000s as coconut milk moved from specialist shops to every supermarket shelf.
